title: From Creative Risks to Purpose-Driven Business: Sybil Steele's Journey
guestBio: Sybil Steele is a powerhouse creative entrepreneur who has evolved from filmmaker and global brand consultant to wellness pioneer. She's the Founder of S'eau Prima skincare, Co-Founder of Repegs payment app and Solento Tequila, and a mother raising two young adults.
excerpt: Creative entrepreneur Sybil Steele shares her evolution from filmmaker to wellness pioneer, revealing how motherhood and intuition guided her journey to building multiple purpose-driven businesses. This conversation is packed with wisdom on reinvention, showing up authentically, and creating work that truly matters.
metaDescription: Creative entrepreneur Sybil Steele shares her journey from filmmaker to wellness pioneer, building S'eau Prima, Repegs, and Solento Tequila with purpose.
keyTakeaways: Personal transformation and motherhood can be powerful catalysts for entrepreneurial pivots and finding your true purpose.
Showing up as your whole authentic self in business creates deeper connections and more meaningful work.
Building businesses rooted in wellness and self-care rituals can transform both your life and your customers' experiences.
Community-inspired solutions like local payment apps can address real-world problems while fostering connection.
Balancing multiple ventures requires intentional focus on what truly matters and aligns with your values.
Creative risks become less scary when they're aligned with your deeper sense of purpose and intuition.
The journey of reinvention is ongoing and requires courage to let go of previous identities that no longer serve you.

pullQuotes.text: The creative process isn't just about making something beautiful—it's about showing up as your whole self and doing work that matters.

timestamps.label: Sybil's evolution from filmmaker to entrepreneur
The story behind S'eau Prima skincare
Creating Repegs payment app for community
Solento Tequila and slow sipping philosophy

content:
What happens when you give yourself permission to completely reinvent your career—not just once, but multiple times? Sybil Steele knows firsthand. In this powerful conversation with Aimee Rickabus, Sybil opens up about her remarkable evolution from filmmaker and global brand consultant to wellness pioneer and multi-business founder.

Sybil's journey isn't just about changing careers; it's about the courage to follow your intuition even when it leads you down unexpected paths. As the founder of S'eau Prima skincare, co-founder of Repegs payment app, and co-founder of Solento Tequila, she's built a portfolio of businesses that reflect her deepest values and serve her community in meaningful ways.

The Power of Authentic Reinvention

One of the most striking aspects of Sybil's story is how motherhood became a catalyst for transformation. Rather than viewing her role as a mother of two young adults as something that might limit her professional possibilities, she embraced it as a source of clarity and purpose. This shift in perspective didn't happen overnight—it required her to shed old identities that no longer served her and step into a more authentic version of herself.

The creative process, as Sybil explains, isn't just about making something beautiful or commercially viable. It's about showing up as your whole self and creating work that matters. This philosophy permeates every venture she's launched, from the self-care rituals embedded in S'eau Prima to the community-focused vision behind Repegs.

Building Wellness Into Business

S'eau Prima represents more than just another skincare brand—it's Sybil's response to recognizing that true wellness starts with how we care for ourselves daily. The brand is rooted in self-care rituals that go beyond surface-level beauty to address deeper needs for grounding and presence. This approach reflects Sybil's understanding that modern women need products and practices that honor their complexity and support their well-being holistically.

What makes S'eau Prima particularly compelling is how it emerged from Sybil's personal journey rather than market research or trend analysis. She created what she needed first, then discovered that other women were hungry for the same authentic approach to self-care. This inside-out method of business development is something many entrepreneurs struggle with, but Sybil demonstrates how powerful it can be when you trust your instincts.

Community-Centered Innovation

The story behind Repegs, Sybil's local payment app, illustrates her commitment to addressing real community needs through technology. Rather than creating another generic fintech solution, she identified a specific problem within her community and developed a tool that fosters connection while solving practical challenges. This approach to entrepreneurship—starting with community and working outward—offers a refreshing alternative to the typical startup mentality of scaling fast and asking questions later.

Repegs represents the kind of thoughtful innovation that comes from deeply understanding your environment and the people you serve. It's a reminder that some of the most meaningful business opportunities aren't found in Silicon Valley trend reports but in the everyday experiences of real communities.

Balancing Multiple Ventures

Managing multiple businesses while raising two young adults might seem overwhelming, but Sybil approaches this challenge with intentionality and grace. Her secret isn't perfect time management or superhuman energy—it's clarity about what matters most and the willingness to make decisions based on her values rather than external expectations.

This balance extends to her involvement with Solento Tequila and its slow sipping ethos. Even in the spirits industry, Sybil has found ways to infuse her ventures with meaning and mindfulness. The slow sipping philosophy isn't just a marketing concept—it reflects her broader approach to life and business, emphasizing quality, presence, and intentional enjoyment over rushed consumption.

The Courage to Take Creative Risks

Perhaps the most inspiring aspect of Sybil's journey is how she's redefined what it means to take creative risks. Rather than viewing risk as something to minimize or manage, she's embraced it as an essential part of living authentically. Her willingness to leave behind successful but unfulfilling work to pursue ventures aligned with her values demonstrates the kind of courage that many women in business need to develop.

Creative risks become less frightening when they're rooted in purpose rather than ego or external validation. Sybil's story shows us that the biggest risk isn't failure—it's staying in situations that don't allow you to show up as your whole self or contribute meaningfully to the world around you.

For women navigating their own transitions, whether in business, motherhood, or personal growth, Sybil's journey offers both inspiration and practical wisdom. Her experience reminds us that reinvention isn't a one-time event but an ongoing process of alignment, and that our most powerful work often emerges from our willingness to honor both our evolution and our community's needs.

The path Sybil has carved demonstrates that you don't have to choose between commercial success and meaningful impact. When you build businesses from a place of authenticity and service, you create something that sustains both you and the people you serve—and that's the kind of work that truly matters.

quiz.title: What's Your Reinvention Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally approach transformation and change in your life and career. Your results will reveal insights inspired by Sybil Steele's powerful journey of authentic reinvention.

questions.question: When you think about making a major life or career change, what feels most important to you?

options.text: Following my gut instinct, even if I can't explain it logically to others
Ensuring the change aligns with my core values and serves a greater purpose
Finding ways the change can benefit my community or solve real problems
Making sure I can show up as my authentic self in this new direction

questions.question: How do you typically respond when you realize something in your life isn't working anymore?

options.text: I'm ready to blow it up and start fresh—I love the thrill of creating something completely new
I carefully evaluate what needs to change and create a plan that honors my bigger vision
I look for ways to reimagine it that could also help or inspire other people
I ask myself what version of this would feel most true to who I'm becoming

questions.question: What energizes you most about the idea of entrepreneurship or creating something new?

options.text: The freedom to follow my curiosity wherever it leads, without having to justify every decision
Building something meaningful that reflects my values and makes a real difference
Creating solutions that bring people together and strengthen communities
Having the space to integrate all parts of myself into my work, not just one dimension

questions.question: When you're feeling stuck or unclear about your next move, you usually:

options.text: Take a creative risk or try something completely different to shake things up
Reconnect with my deeper purpose and ask what would feel most meaningful
Talk to people in my community to understand what's needed and where I can contribute
Step back and reflect on what feels authentic versus what I think I 'should' do

questions.question: How do you view the relationship between your personal growth and your professional life?

options.text: My personal evolution naturally leads to new creative and professional possibilities
They should reinforce each other—my work should support who I want to become
Both grow stronger when I focus on how I can serve and connect with others
They're inseparable—I can't compartmentalize different parts of myself

questions.question: What draws you most to successful women entrepreneurs like Sybil Steele?

options.text: Their willingness to completely reinvent themselves and take bold creative leaps
How they've built businesses that reflect their values and create positive impact
Their ability to identify community needs and create innovative solutions
The way they've integrated their whole selves into their work and leadership

results.title: The Intuitive Pioneer
results.description: You trust your inner compass above all else, even when it leads you down unexpected paths. Like Sybil, you're willing to completely reimagine your life when something doesn't feel aligned anymore. You see transformation as an adventure rather than something to fear, and you're naturally drawn to creating something entirely new rather than tweaking what already exists. This episode will inspire you to embrace your next bold leap with even more confidence.

results.title: The Purposeful Builder
results.description: You approach change strategically, always connecting your next move to your deeper values and long-term vision. You're skilled at taking life's pivotal moments—like motherhood or career shifts—and using them as fuel for meaningful transformation. You don't just want success; you want your work to matter and serve others authentically. Sybil's story of building multiple purpose-driven businesses will show you how to scale your impact while staying true to your core values.

results.title: The Creative Connector
results.description: You see opportunities everywhere to bring people together and solve real problems through innovation. Your superpower is spotting gaps in your community and creating solutions that foster deeper connections. You're naturally collaborative and believe the best ideas come from understanding what people actually need. This episode will resonate with your community-first approach and inspire new ways to blend creativity with meaningful impact.

results.title: The Authentic Evolutionist
results.description: You understand that reinvention isn't a one-time event—it's an ongoing journey of becoming more yourself. You're comfortable letting go of old identities when they no longer serve you, and you prioritize showing up as your whole, authentic self in everything you do. You believe that personal transformation and professional success go hand in hand. Sybil's journey of continuous evolution while staying grounded in authenticity will deeply inspire your own path forward.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Let's have some fun. Today I am honored to welcome an extraordinary leader, creative visionary, and global force for change, Sybil Steele. Sybil is the co-founder and CEO of Repe, a local perks and payment platform. She's also the co-founder of Suprima Skincare and Sento Organic Tequila. Named a woman to watch in tech, Sybil premiered Mothers of the Atlas at the United Nations, using storytelling and technology to spotlight maternal healthcare around the globe.

She's lived and worked in Tokyo, Paris, Bali, Australia, and across the US. And she brings a deeply international lens to every brand system and movement she touches. As a director and storyteller, she's led transformative projects like The Gift of Robin Limb and 100 Wells for Ethiopia. And she's been at the forefront of major International Women's Days campaigns advocating for systemic change.

Beyond business, Sybil is the mother of two incredible young women and a fierce champion for global women's empowerment, celebrate voices, and build companies that create lasting, meaningful impact. She's had a first look at my new book, and I could not be more excited to have this conversation. Let's dive in.

Hi, Sybil. Oh my gosh, I'm so honored to have you here.

**Sybil:** Thank you, Aimee. I was just saying, where have you been my whole life?

**Aimee:** Where have you been my whole life? I can't wait to do a million things with you.

**Sybil:** Let's do it. There's so much to do. We have a lot of work to do as women right now.

**Aimee:** We do. And I'm here for it. So grateful to meet you in person and get to have this conversation with you. Let's start with your journey. You're the founder and CEO of Repe and you've also launched Sento Tequila and Suprima Skincare. Can you walk me through how you did each of these brands? How did they come to life? What connects with them?

**Sybil:** Yeah, that's an interesting question. I have been in film and media production. I was in that for 20 years. And a lot of what I did was fashion photography and film making and women's documentaries and highlighting women's issues. I was always in service to women and always trying to elevate women whether it was in photography through the female gaze as opposed to through the male lens of shooting women and other films where we could highlight women's work.

For many years I was in service and basically that was my product and eventually in 2017 I moved into product which is the Sento Tequila and the Suprima skincare. It's really a powerful face mist. Our body makes it from our white blood cells. It's made from hypochlorous acid. And it repairs and defends our skin, but it's all natural.

I started using it as skincare. Because I was such a busy mom of two and I was running two companies and I was running the household, I started using it and my skin just had a full glow up. I didn't have time to go and do the lasers and all the things. So I was like, I'm just going to use this run and gun, get my stuff done, accomplish my goals. And so I brought it to a premium skincare line that I launched in 2021.

For me it's easy. It's on the go. It's powerful. We say powerful beauty within. It's all about celebrating the uniqueness of women and activating ourselves. And then the Sento tequila is with my ex-husband. He runs the day-to-day. But I don't know if you drink tequila, but tequila is the only thing I drink when I drink. I rarely drink, but when I do, it's tequila. And organic tequila, it's right on point for me. So it's no calories, no sugar. It's organic. And women are 80% of our consumer buying decisions. A lot of women are drinking tequila. So it was a natural fit to also speak to the female audience who really would appreciate this product.

And Repe is all about community. And as we know, women are at the forefront of building community and really being the communications and the liaison and the word of mouth for everyone. So Repe came about by nurturing and cultivating community and bringing everybody into creating success together. How can we support each other?

**Aimee:** I think Repe is the most abstract of the things that you're working on right now. So tell us a little bit more about how Repe works and how it helps to build community.

**Sybil:** Yeah. So, it's an app and basically there's a digital wallet in it and you pay different merchants through it. So, it can be anyone from restaurants to spas to your local surfboard shop to a masseuse or anyone who's a local merchant is allowed to come on the app as a business and what it does it connects you with all the locals. So each county gets their own Repe app. And we launched in San Diego about almost a year ago and they get access to special offers, curated experiences, things that you normally don't get to see or find in the community. And what it does, it creates a stronger relationship between the locals and the merchants.

You might save on something and then part of that savings goes to a local charity. So all the money stays in the local economy and 67 cents of every dollar spent usually stays in the local economy spent at local merchants, right? So that's awesome. We're encouraging people don't shop on the big corporations. Let's really support the merchants. They need our help now more than ever. Supporting small businesses locals are what keep small businesses afloat. So, we're really encouraging that and just trying to strengthen the relationships, elevate everybody in innovative tech ways where we can do exchanges economically and yeah, it's really going well and we're launching outside of Boston next Wednesday. So, we're expanding and we're going to expand into different markets nationwide.

**Aimee:** When will you be in the OC?

**Sybil:** Whenever. Call next week.

**Aimee:** Let's get it done. Yeah, we're so ready for this here. I love the idea of keeping our money local in our local economy and helping to support our small local businesses because really as the world and technology rolls on, it's hard to support your local businesses because the big ones are getting so much market share right now. We're spending so many of our dollars with the big guys.

**Sybil:** Yeah, I agree. I mean, I had to stop completely and it's hard, right? Because it's convenient and what might not be convenient is going to a local merchant, but they need our help. So, if you can just make that effort, we try and make it as easy as possible. Communications wise, digitally, we have hyper focused notifications, local notifications that go out to people. And 93% of our users say that they're discovering new places on the app.

So, if you wake up on a Friday morning, you're like, "Where do I want to go to dinner tonight, but I don't want to go to the same place I've been going?" You just scroll through, you see what's available, you see that synergy between they're saying, "Hey, we support the locals. Come support us and let's have that symbiotic relationship." And that's what's cool about the app because it's a combination of Venmo meets Instagram. So it really does have this social media aspect to it where people can post pictures and you can really get more of a local flavor visually of what's going on around you which is so cool.

**Aimee:** Because you're not going to see that post on Instagram. Really? It's going to get lost in the algorithm. And is that what you're looking for?

**Sybil:** Yeah. I think it's so cool. I actually really just got to take a look at it and I think it looks awesome. Super proud of you. Good job. And I know you have a background in tech, so you probably clearly understand it's scalable. It's something that can benefit people in a really impactful way. And there's purpose and it's been one of my favorite products jobs that I've ever had. It's really neat to see women coming into the apps and the technology space. It's really exciting for me because when women bring their creativity to business, it's usually to improve the world in some way and it's so lovely to see that purpose.

**Aimee:** Yeah, good job. Love that. Can't wait to get Orange County on it. So, you've had a career rooted in brand building, strategy, and creative leadership. How do you approach launching something new in a way that feels both aligned and impactful?

**Sybil:** Well, I guess that comes from the origin of where the product comes from anyway, right? So, I feel like the alignment has to be there in order to launch a product in terms of do I like this? Do I need it? Do I see value in it? Does it bring value to other people? So the alignment is, I've never created a product that I didn't believe in or offer a service that I didn't believe in or figure out a way how I could elevate or position women within that ecosystem or in that world that we're building. So alignment has to be there.

And I think always building something that's going to have impact, right? Because profit's great and you have to have that in business. But for me, everything that drives me is purpose. It's like, why are we doing this? How does this help people? How does it help community? Who's going to love this? Who does this bring joy to? It brings me joy. Who else is like me who wants to see something like this?

And with Suprima, when people are like, "Oh my gosh, this fixed something on my skin or I've been dealing with this skin issue for years and it helps to alleviate that problem." And you see the joy in people. It's like that's exactly why it was created because it helped other people and myself with so many skin issues.

**Aimee:** I think this is the way women approach business in general is we come as problem solvers and the products that we bring forward solve problems. Have you heard of the contrast between the masculine economy and the feminine economy?

**Sybil:** No. Please tell me.

**Aimee:** Yeah, there's this framework of what are the values in a masculine economy? What are the values in a feminine economy? And they're opposites. So if you look at this wheel, I'll send it to you. They're opposites of each other. And not to say that the masculine economy and the feminine economy can't merge and mix like anything that's higher value probably brings balance to the world if we had an equal balance of those two things. Collaboration or is it merit based right but how can you merge that and is it abundance or is it scarcity so those kind of things it's like we might have some limitations here but actually there's enough for everybody here there's enough in the pie so I try and build things through that lens as and companies and teams through that lens of the feminine economy. We've got the masculine economy. It's totally embedded in everything that we do. So, how do we bring in more of that collaboration and abundance and communication?

**Sybil:** Absolutely. So aligned on this. So aligned. That's so much about what the book is about, bringing, right now I think it's something very sad. I think female-led companies get 3% of all capital. We're watering 97% of men's ideas and 3% of women's ideas. How can we have balance on the planet? We really work for that balance.

**Aimee:** Yeah. And I really feel this is the fourth industrial revolution. This is my call to action. It is women, we have to take a seat at the table. We need to be equals so that we can bring balance in the way that we see the world and the way that we bring purpose to the things that we do and the products that we create. How we're always thinking about how this will impact the next seven generations, how this will impact our families, our children. We're not just trying to make money. We care about impact and we care about purpose. And money comes with purpose, right? If you're fulfilling your purpose and you're making smart business decisions, it comes it's a byproduct.

**Sybil:** Yeah. Exactly. Yeah. It's a byproduct. By the way, congratulations on your book.

**Aimee:** Thank you so much. Which just launched today and I feel so honored to be with you and that energy of launching your book and that it went number one on Amazon the day that it launched.

**Sybil:** I feel really grateful to be here in basking in your glow and hard work. I am so grateful to have you here today. I feel like the universe has conspired in many ways and I feel like us being together and having this conversation today is very much a big part of that. I feel like there's going to be a very big community that will hover around this idea this movement this idea of elevating women in the next industrial revolution that we're in right now. Do you feel like you have a lot of female leaders around you in this area in your community that are similar in mindset?

**Aimee:** I really do. A lot of the women that I'm in We Bank, if you're not a member of We Bank, you might want to.

**Sybil:** Are you really? You should. Yeah, you should look into it. It's kind of sad that we've lost EEI but I hope that We Bank will survive regardless because the cooperation that can come from having a group of women work together, very well-connected, very successful women, having a network together is the key. It really is. All these women are there because of the merit. They've done the work and they know what they're doing. And to have a group of women like that is fantastic. But to actually be rising up into a place of leadership for women, no, I'd love to join you. I would love to join you.

**Aimee:** We're always learning, right? Right now, I just started working with a leadership and growth coach. Her name is Linda Lucas and she's based out of Portland. I just met her last week. I was on a thought leadership trip up to the Pacific Northwest and I met her last week and I just immediately connected with her. It was not like love at first sight, but just a pure total alignment and understanding. And she's been helping me figure out what are my strengths and weaknesses and what are some of the spots that I could work on as a leader and it's uncomfortable and hard work. It's a little scary and sometimes it's vulnerable and so I'm really learning to lean into that space and be like, okay, actually this is where I need to really look at things and reconfigure them for my teams to lead properly.

**Sybil:** Yeah, I know. Realizing writing the book was the easy part, that actually launching the book and putting it out into the world was the most vulnerable part. It really was for me to overcome the fear that comes with just being visible and that was the biggest one. But I'm embracing it and I just was like, Aimee, just have a beginner's mind. Let's go and you don't know what you're going to experience today. I woke up this morning just a regular girl and I'm going to get to go to bed a bestselling author and you don't always get days like that. So that's a good day.

**Aimee:** Congratulations. I mean that's a big day. That's a big day. And a lot of people have knowledge, right? But they don't share it. So if people can put your knowledge into action, that's a powerful thing. Passing it on. And because of the technology that we're in right now, the tech era that we're in, there's never been an easier time to take your knowledge, put it into a book or an ebook or a course. Do it. Do the things. Pass the knowledge along. If you have it, share it.

**Sybil:** Yeah, we need it. And that's why I'm a little bit intimidated and shy sometimes to come out and talk about myself because we always want to elevate other people. We always want to highlight other people.

**Aimee:** So, Repe is such a powerful and timely platform. I feel like we've kind of covered it though. Do you want to talk more about what inspired its creation and how do you see it evolving?

**Sybil:** I think basically across the nation. But I think what sort of traits do we need? What sort of resources do we need? What kind of support do we need? And we'll be fundraising. So, probably by the time this launches, we are in a fund raise. That is something that's new for me and I'm super excited about it. If you would have said to me three years ago, somebody you need to raise 5 million for a company, I would be like, but I'm a creative director, right? I want to make things look pretty and I want to talk about them in a beautiful way. And so I've evolved to be CEO and what do you do when you're in that position? You stay close to the money. You make sure that you have the operational runway to run, to grow, to scale. And so once I decided to step into that space, I was like, it's a hell yes. There is no going back from that.

So, I think that's a new skill and test for me and it's pitching to a lot of men in the fintech industry. That's a new industry for me. I'm not scared of it. I just want to be prepared. I want to know as much as I possibly can. And also, I surround myself with really smart people. I know what I don't know. I know what I'm good at. I know what my superpowers are. I know where I can use that extra support. And so that's just been a massive initiative this year for me to get uncomfortable so I can get comfortable again and challenge myself. So taking Repe to the next level is a personal thing. It's a professional thing. It's a team thing. It's a tech thing. It's a community thing. It's so exciting.

**Aimee:** It's so exciting. Being on the verge like that of doing something really big that's really outside of your comfort zone. But once you set these high goals for we do we as women there are a group of us that set these very high goals for ourselves and it's so much fun to support each other in those goals. You let me know if you need help raising that money. I love raising money. It's super fun. And it's one of the things I'd really love to get into as my movement grows to have a separate part of this movement that's about capital 3% of all VC funding goes to women led companies. It is sad. That is really sad.

**Sybil:** It is sad. It's pathetic. I mean, and it's a hard time right now to raise money. Yeah, it really is. I mean, 30% would be sad, but three is like, are you joking? Are we even on the radar here? No, we're not. There's a table for 100 and we get three seats basically is what it looks like right now. And that's not acceptable. And I think that there are a lot of women that are working on this problem right now. And I'd love to see this correct within the next 12 months because technology.

**Aimee:** Yeah. I'm down. Yeah. Let's make it happen. That's another deal. We've got four deals going. I think that's plenty of time, but because technology is moving so quickly, I don't think we have a whole lot of time to wait for a seat at their table. I think we're going to have to build our own when it comes to capital. And I have a good idea for that.

**Sybil:** Oh, great. I can't wait to hear it. I know. I'm working on it right now. Without capital, capital is the water that grows the garden. And without the capital, we cannot grow. We will not see these things come to fruition. And Sento is a male-led company and we had a line. We had a line ready to give us checks.

**Aimee:** Of course you did. That was easy.

**Sybil:** That was easy. Yeah. Not to say that the people behind it didn't deserve it. They absolutely do. But there was a wait list. That's amazing. And it's fun. And it's not to say that just because it's female-led, we don't have a line out the door because I believe that within the next 12 months, we can shift the mindset and I think we can also create the table that allows us to have standing room only and waits to get on these projects as women and men both see that when women have ideas it's because we see a gap in a market and we are the purchasers. The average woman of the household buys 80% of the goods within her home. So when we see a gap in the market, it's usually a really good sign that this is going to be a very viable product that we're bringing to market. And female-led companies are more successful. I mean, the data shows it. So there is all of that to back us up as well.

**Aimee:** I think that the shift is coming swiftly now. I don't think it's far now for us.

**Sybil:** Well, I'm inspired to hear all of that from you today. So I'll walk away being like, we've got this.

**Aimee:** Oh, we've got this. And I'll put you in touch with some people. I know some people that are interested in these things as well.

**Sybil:** Yeah, absolutely. Let's talk business building. So, a lot of my girls, a lot of our listeners out there maybe haven't done anything yet. So, I really want to inspire them and I want to educate them. So you get an idea and then what did the early days look like? Did you raise capital? Did you have a formal business plan? How did it get going?

**Aimee:** Well, Sento started as an all male company and then I came in and I was like, but hey, women drink tequila. A lot of women drink tequila. Again, back to the health benefits, so to speak. 80% of consumer buying decisions. We're in Whole Foods, right? So, there you go. They can afford it. It's the price point's over $60 a bottle. So let's get it in a position where it's speaking to the female audience and they understand it and they love it and they appreciate the product that we've built for them. So that's how I got involved.

I think that you always need a plan, but be okay for a plan to change. Don't be scared to go in an industry you don't know anything about. Sometimes I think that's better because that naivety if you knew all the things behind skin care or the alcohol industry or tech you might be weighing the risk you might say oh I don't know that sounds a little intimidating so I think just forget all of that and create what you would create stand behind it and surround yourself again with really smart people I think people who have your back that you trust who have a track record of doing great things. So, it's all really about your network, too. It's about who you have to support you, resources, and really cultivating those relationships.

It really is a state of mind, right? Creating your own business is not for everyone. It takes a ton of resilience. One of the things the growth coach, Linda, is teaching me right now is thrive in VUCA. Do you know what that is?

**Sybil:** I don't.

**Aimee:** I said thrive or survive. She said thrive in VUCA. So it's volatility, chaos, ambiguity, and uncertainty. And I did a little side and I was like, okay, but I learn quick and I can recalibrate quick and I'm like, okay, not survive in it, thrive in it.
